name: Cacio e Pepe
description: A Roman pasta classic — just pasta water, Pecorino Romano, and cracked black pepper. The magic is in the technique: the starchy water emulsifies the cheese into a silky sauce.
ingredients:
- 200g spaghetti or tonnarelli
- 80g Pecorino Romano, finely grated
- 20g Parmesan, finely grated
- 2 tsp freshly cracked black pepper
- Salt for pasta water
instructions:
1. Bring a pot of lightly salted water to a boil. Cook pasta until just under al dente, reserving 1 cup pasta water.
2. Toast black pepper in a dry pan over medium heat for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
3. Add ½ cup pasta water to the pan, bring to a simmer.
4. Add pasta directly to the pan and toss to coat.
5. Remove from heat. Add cheese gradually while tossing and adding pasta water as needed to form a creamy sauce.
6. Serve immediately with extra cheese and pepper.
tips: Use very starchy pasta water — it's the secret to the sauce emulsifying. Never add cheese over direct heat or it will clump.

name: Spaghetti Carbonara
description: The original Roman carbonara — no cream, ever. Just guanciale, eggs, Pecorino, and pepper working together into something impossibly rich.
ingredients:
- 200g spaghetti
- 120g guanciale (or pancetta), cubed
- 2 large eggs + 2 egg yolks
- 60g Pecorino Romano, grated
- 20g Parmesan, grated
- Generous black pepper
- Salt for pasta water
instructions:
1. Cook pasta in salted water until al dente, reserving 1 cup pasta water.
2. Render guanciale in a cold pan over medium heat until crispy. Remove pan from heat.
3. Whisk eggs, yolks, and cheeses together. Season generously with black pepper.
4. Add hot pasta to guanciale pan (off heat). Toss to combine.
5. Add egg mixture and toss quickly, adding pasta water splash by splash until sauce is creamy and coats every strand.
6. Serve immediately with extra cheese and pepper.
tips: The pan must be off the heat when you add the egg mixture, or the eggs will scramble. Work fast and use the pasta water to control consistency.

name: Mushroom Risotto
description: Creamy, earthy risotto with a mix of mushrooms — porcini for depth, cremini for body. The constant stirring is meditative, not a chore.
ingredients:
- 300g Arborio rice
- 30g dried porcini mushrooms
- 300g cremini mushrooms, sliced
- 1 shallot, minced
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 120ml dry white wine
- 1L warm vegetable broth
- 60g Parmesan, grated
- 30g butter
- 2 tbsp olive oil
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Soak dried porcini in 200ml hot water for 15 minutes. Drain, reserving liquid. Chop porcini.
2. Sauté cremini mushrooms in olive oil until golden. Set aside.
3. In the same pan, soften shallot in butter. Add garlic, then rice. Toast rice for 2 minutes.
4. Add wine and stir until absorbed.
5. Add warm broth one ladle at a time, stirring constantly until each addition is absorbed.
6. After 20 minutes, add porcini soaking liquid (strained) and chopped porcini.
7. Stir in sautéed cremini, Parmesan, and a knob of butter. Season and serve.
tips: The strained porcini soaking liquid adds incredible depth — don't skip it. Leave the risotto slightly looser than you think; it tightens as it sits.

name: Tiramisu
description: Classic tiramisu — layers of espresso-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream dusted with cocoa. Made the traditional way, with raw egg yolks and no gelatin.
ingredients:
- 300g savoiardi (ladyfingers)
- 500g mascarpone, room temperature
- 4 large eggs, separated
- 100g sugar
- 300ml strong espresso, cooled
- 60ml Marsala or rum
- Unsweetened cocoa powder for dusting
instructions:
1. Whisk egg yolks and sugar until pale and thick, about 5 minutes.
2. Fold in mascarpone until smooth.
3. Beat egg whites to stiff peaks. Gently fold into mascarpone mixture.
4. Combine espresso and Marsala in a shallow dish.
5. Quickly dip each ladyfinger and arrange in a single layer in your dish.
6. Spread half the mascarpone cream over the ladyfingers.
7. Repeat with a second layer of dipped ladyfingers and remaining cream.
8. Refrigerate at least 6 hours or overnight. Dust with cocoa before serving.
tips: Don't over-soak the ladyfingers — a quick 1-second dip on each side is enough. Rest overnight for best results.

name: Miso Ramen
description: A rich, umami-forward miso broth with wavy ramen noodles, soft-boiled egg, and toppings. Built on a tare you make once and use forever.
tagline: A bowl that actually warms you up
ingredients:
- 150g fresh ramen noodles
- 4 cups chicken or pork broth
- 3 tbsp white miso paste
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tbsp mirin
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- 2 soft-boiled eggs
- 100g corn kernels
- 2 sheets nori
- 2 stalks green onion, sliced
- 1 tbsp butter
instructions:
1. Make soft-boiled eggs: boil for 6.5 minutes, cool in ice water, peel.
2. Heat broth in a pot. Whisk in miso, soy, mirin, and sesame oil. Do not boil after adding miso.
3. Cook ramen noodles separately per package instructions. Drain.
4. In a small pan, heat butter and sauté corn until slightly caramelized.
5. Divide noodles into bowls. Ladle hot broth over.
6. Top with halved egg, corn, nori, and green onion.
tips: Never boil the broth after adding miso — it kills the live cultures and flattens the flavor. The butter-corn is the detail that makes it restaurant-quality.

name: Matcha Latte
description: A smooth, frothy matcha latte with just the right balance of earthy and sweet. Ceremonial-grade matcha makes all the difference.
ingredients:
- 2 tsp ceremonial-grade matcha powder
- 60ml hot water (not boiling, ~80°C)
- 200ml oat milk (or milk of choice)
- 1–2 tsp maple syrup or honey
instructions:
1. Sift matcha into a mug or bowl.
2. Add hot water and whisk in a W motion until fully smooth and slightly frothy.
3. Warm and froth the oat milk until creamy.
4. Add sweetener to the matcha shot.
5. Pour frothed milk over and stir gently.
tips: Sifting prevents clumps. Use water around 80°C — boiling water makes matcha bitter.

name: Miso Glazed Eggplant
description: Silky roasted eggplant with a sweet-savory miso glaze, broiled until caramelized. A Japanese classic that feels indulgent despite being completely plant-based.
ingredients:
- 2 Japanese eggplants, halved lengthwise
- 3 tbsp white miso
- 2 tbsp mirin
- 1 tbsp sake or dry sherry
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp sesame oil
- Sesame seeds and green onion to garnish
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 220°C. Score eggplant flesh in a crosshatch pattern.
2. Brush with sesame oil and roast cut-side down for 15 minutes.
3. Meanwhile, whisk miso, mirin, sake, and sugar until smooth.
4. Flip eggplant, brush generously with miso glaze.
5. Broil for 5–8 minutes until glaze is caramelized and bubbling.
6. Garnish with sesame seeds and green onion.
tips: Scoring the flesh helps the glaze penetrate deeper. Don't skip the broil — it transforms the glaze.

name: Matcha Mochi
description: Soft, chewy mochi with a pronounced matcha flavor. Filled with red bean paste or left plain, it's a simple and deeply satisfying Japanese sweet.
ingredients:
- 200g glutinous rice flour (mochiko)
- 2 tbsp matcha powder
- 180ml water
- 80g sugar
- Cornstarch for dusting
- 200g red bean paste (anko), optional
instructions:
1. Whisk rice flour, matcha, sugar, and water until smooth.
2. Pour into a microwave-safe bowl. Microwave on high for 2 minutes, stir.
3. Microwave again for 1–2 minutes until dough is cooked through and glossy.
4. Dust a work surface generously with cornstarch.
5. Turn out mochi and work quickly — divide into 8 pieces.
6. Flatten each piece, add a ball of anko in the center, and pinch to seal.
7. Roll in cornstarch to prevent sticking.
tips: Work fast — mochi hardens as it cools. Keep your hands dusted with cornstarch. It can also be made in a steamer over the stove.

name: Edamame with Sea Salt
description: A barely-a-recipe snack that's somehow always the best thing on the table. Blanched edamame, flaky sea salt, and nothing else.
tagline: The best snack you don't have to make
ingredients:
- 300g frozen edamame in pods
- Flaky sea salt (Maldon or similar)
- Optional: pinch of togarashi or yuzu zest
instructions:
1. Bring a pot of salted water to a boil.
2. Add frozen edamame and cook for 3–5 minutes until bright green and tender.
3. Drain and transfer to a bowl.
4. Immediately sprinkle generously with flaky sea salt.
5. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: The pods should be slightly salty themselves — use aggressively salted water. Eat by pressing the beans out with your teeth.

name: Dalgona Coffee
description: The whipped coffee that took the internet by storm — beaten instant coffee foam floated on cold milk. Requires effort but no equipment.
tagline: Whip it until it's worth it
ingredients:
- 2 tbsp instant coffee
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 2 tbsp hot water
- 200ml cold milk (any kind)
instructions:
1. Combine instant coffee, sugar, and hot water in a bowl.
2. Whip with a hand mixer or vigorously by hand until pale, thick, and stiff-peaked — about 5 minutes.
3. Fill a glass with ice and pour in cold milk.
4. Spoon whipped coffee foam on top.
5. Stir before drinking.
tips: It only works with instant coffee, not brewed. By hand takes 10+ minutes — a hand mixer takes 2.

name: Beef Bibimbap
description: A composed rice bowl with seasoned vegetables, marinated beef, a fried egg, and a generous spoonful of gochujang. Everything in harmony.
tagline: Mix it all up — that's the point
ingredients:
- 2 cups cooked short-grain rice
- 150g ground beef
- 100g spinach
- 100g bean sprouts
- 1 medium carrot, julienned
- 1 zucchini, julienned
- 2 eggs
- 2 tbsp gochujang
- 2 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tbsp sesame oil
- 1 tbsp sesame seeds
- Sugar, garlic, salt
instructions:
1. Season beef with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and a pinch of sugar. Cook in a hot pan and set aside.
2. Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with soy sauce and sesame oil.
3. Sauté each vegetable separately with a little oil, garlic, and salt.
4. Fry eggs sunny-side up.
5. Arrange rice in bowls. Place vegetables and beef in sections around the bowl.
6. Top with fried egg. Serve with gochujang on the side.
tips: Traditionally made in a hot stone pot (dolsot) for crispy rice at the bottom — if you have one, heat it before adding rice.

name: Gochujang Butter Pasta
description: A pantry-raid weeknight pasta: gochujang and butter create a spicy, umami sauce that coats pasta perfectly. Controversial fusion that actually works.
ingredients:
- 200g spaghetti or linguine
- 3 tbsp gochujang
- 3 tbsp butter
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tbsp honey
- ½ cup pasta water
- Parmesan to serve
- Green onion to garnish
instructions:
1. Cook pasta in salted water until al dente. Reserve ½ cup pasta water.
2. Melt butter in a large pan over medium heat. Add garlic and cook 1 minute.
3. Add gochujang and stir to combine with butter.
4. Add soy sauce and honey.
5. Add pasta directly to pan with a splash of pasta water. Toss to coat.
6. Add more pasta water to loosen as needed.
7. Serve with Parmesan and green onion.
tips: Adjust gochujang to your heat tolerance. The pasta water is essential — it creates the glossy sauce.

name: Kimchi Pancakes
description: Crispy, savory kimchi pajeon — fermented kimchi and batter fried into golden pancakes. The older and sourer the kimchi, the better.
tagline: Use the kimchi that's been in the back of the fridge
ingredients:
- 150g well-fermented kimchi, roughly chopped
- 100g all-purpose flour
- 1 tbsp cornstarch
- 120ml cold water
- 2 tbsp kimchi juice
- 2 green onions, sliced
- Neutral oil for frying
- Soy sauce and rice vinegar for dipping
instructions:
1. Combine flour, cornstarch, water, and kimchi juice in a bowl. Mix until just combined (some lumps are ok).
2. Fold in chopped kimchi and green onions.
3. Heat a generous amount of oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat.
4. Pour batter into pan to form pancake(s). Press down to flatten.
5. Cook 3–4 minutes until golden and crispy. Flip and cook another 3 minutes.
6. Mix soy sauce and rice vinegar for dipping.
tips: High heat and enough oil is the secret to crispy pajeon. Don't move it around — let it form a crust.

name: French Crêpes
description: Thin, delicate crêpes — the base for sweet or savory toppings. The batter rests overnight for the most tender result.
ingredients:
- 2 large eggs
- 250ml milk
- 125g all-purpose flour
- 1 tbsp butter, melted
- 1 tsp sugar
- Pinch of salt
- Butter for cooking
instructions:
1. Blend all ingredients until smooth. Refrigerate batter for at least 1 hour (or overnight).
2. Heat a crêpe pan or non-stick pan over medium heat. Brush with butter.
3. Pour a small ladle of batter and quickly swirl to coat the pan thinly.
4. Cook 1–2 minutes until edges start to lift and surface is set.
5. Flip and cook 30 seconds more.
6. Serve with Nutella, fresh fruit, lemon and sugar, or ham and cheese.
tips: The first crêpe is always a test crêpe — it's for the cook, not the guests. Resting the batter is non-negotiable for tender results.

name: Potato Gratin
description: Gratin dauphinois — thinly sliced potatoes baked in cream with garlic and nutmeg until golden and bubbling. Achingly good alongside anything roasted.
ingredients:
- 800g starchy potatoes, thinly sliced
- 300ml heavy cream
- 200ml whole milk
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tsp nutmeg
- Salt and white pepper
- Butter for the dish
- 50g Gruyère, grated (optional)
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 180°C. Butter a baking dish.
2. Combine cream, milk, garlic, nutmeg, salt, and pepper in a pot. Heat until just warm.
3. Layer potatoes in the dish, overlapping slightly.
4. Pour cream mixture over potatoes — it should just reach the top layer.
5. Cover with foil and bake 40 minutes.
6. Uncover, add Gruyère if using, and bake another 20–25 minutes until golden.
tips: Mandoline-thin slices are key — they cook evenly and absorb the cream. Use a starchy potato like Russet, not waxy.

name: Crème Brûlée
description: Silky vanilla custard with a crackling sugar crust. Requires a torch and a steady hand, but the payoff is one of the great simple pleasures.
tagline: For the love of that crack
ingredients:
- 500ml heavy cream
- 5 large egg yolks
- 100g sugar, plus more for topping
- 1 vanilla bean or 1 tsp vanilla extract
- Pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 160°C. Heat cream with vanilla until just simmering.
2. Whisk egg yolks with sugar until pale.
3. Slowly pour hot cream into yolks while whisking constantly.
4. Strain through a fine sieve. Divide into 4 ramekins.
5. Bake in a water bath for 40–45 minutes until set with a slight wobble.
6. Chill at least 2 hours. Before serving, sprinkle with sugar and torch until caramelized.
tips: The custard should wobble like Jell-O in the center when done — it firms as it chills. Sugar the top right before serving so the crust stays crisp.

name: Baked Brie
description: A whole wheel of brie baked until molten, topped with honey, walnuts, and fresh thyme. The easiest impressive thing you can serve.
ingredients:
- 1 wheel brie (about 250g)
- 2 tbsp honey
- 30g walnuts, roughly chopped
- 4 sprigs fresh thyme
- Pinch of red chili flakes
- Crackers or sliced baguette to serve
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 180°C.
2. Place brie on a small baking sheet or in a small cast iron pan.
3. Score the top in a crosshatch pattern.
4. Top with walnuts and thyme.
5. Drizzle with honey and a pinch of chili flakes.
6. Bake for 15–20 minutes until soft and oozing.
7. Serve immediately with crackers.
tips: If you score the top, the cheese will melt out beautifully. Have something to scoop with ready — it waits for no one once it's out of the oven.

name: Café au Lait
description: Strong brewed coffee with warm, frothy milk — simple, perfect, and better than anything else for a slow Sunday morning.
tagline: The only reason to own a French press
ingredients:
- 180ml strong brewed coffee or café
- 180ml whole milk
- Optional: sugar or sweetener
instructions:
1. Brew strong coffee — French press or moka pot works best.
2. Warm milk in a small saucepan until just below a simmer.
3. Froth the milk with a whisk or milk frother.
4. Pour coffee and warm milk into a wide bowl or large mug simultaneously.
5. Add sugar if desired.
tips: Whole milk froths best. The secret is pouring coffee and milk at the same time — it's a style, not just tradition.

name: Avocado Toast
description: The one that started a thousand arguments. Done right — good bread, ripe avocado, red pepper flakes, and flaky salt — it deserves the attention.
tagline: Do it right or not at all
ingredients:
- 2 slices sourdough or thick-cut toast
- 1 ripe avocado
- Juice of ½ lemon
- Flaky sea salt
- Red pepper flakes
- Optional: 2 eggs, everything bagel seasoning, microgreens
instructions:
1. Toast bread until golden and crispy.
2. Halve avocado and remove pit. Scoop into a bowl.
3. Add lemon juice and mash with a fork — leave some texture.
4. Season generously with salt.
5. Spread on toast.
6. Top with flaky salt, red pepper flakes, and any extras.
tips: Room temperature avocado mashes more evenly. The lemon is mandatory — it prevents browning and brightens everything.

name: Soft Scrambled Eggs
description: Low and slow scrambled eggs — custardy, creamy, barely set. The opposite of dry diner eggs, and better than anything else you can make in 10 minutes.
ingredients:
- 3 large eggs
- 1 tbsp butter
- 1 tbsp crème fraîche or cream cheese
- Flaky salt and chives
instructions:
1. Crack eggs into a cold non-stick pan. Add butter.
2. Turn heat to low. Stir constantly with a rubber spatula.
3. As curds form, pull pan off heat and keep stirring.
4. Return to heat, stir; off heat, stir. Repeat.
5. When eggs are just barely set and glossy, remove from heat.
6. Stir in crème fraîche.
7. Season with flaky salt. Serve immediately on toast.
tips: This is patience cooking. The moment you rush it, you've made regular scrambled eggs. Off-heat is just as important as on-heat.

name: Pan-Seared Salmon
description: Crispy-skinned salmon cooked entirely on the stovetop — golden on the outside, just barely translucent in the center. No oven required.
tagline: The only way to cook salmon
ingredients:
- 2 salmon fillets (about 170g each), skin-on
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 1 tbsp butter
- 2 cloves garlic
- 2 sprigs fresh thyme or rosemary
- Lemon to serve
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Pat salmon dry with paper towels. Season generously with salt and pepper.
2. Heat oil in a cast iron or stainless pan over medium-high until shimmering.
3. Place salmon skin-side down. Press gently with a spatula for 30 seconds.
4. Cook skin-side down for 5–7 minutes until skin is golden and crispy.
5. Flip, add butter, garlic, and thyme to pan. Baste with the butter for 2 minutes.
6. Remove when a thermometer reads 52°C internal, or when flesh is just barely opaque.
7. Rest 2 minutes. Serve with lemon.
tips: Starting skin-side down and not touching it is the key to crispy skin. Don't move the fish. The fish is done when it releases from the pan naturally.

name: Caesar Salad
description: A proper Caesar — anchovy-forward dressing made from scratch, Parmesan snow, and homemade croutons. Better than any bottled version.
tagline: Anchovy is the secret you can't taste
ingredients:
- 2 hearts of Romaine, chopped
- 80g Parmesan, half grated finely + half shaved
- 2 slices thick bread, cubed (for croutons)
- 3 tbsp olive oil
- 2 egg yolks
- 4 anchovy fillets, minced to a paste
- 1 clove garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp Dijon mustard
- 2 tbsp lemon juice
- 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Make croutons: toss bread in olive oil and salt, bake at 200°C for 12 minutes until golden.
2. Whisk egg yolks, anchovy paste, garlic, Dijon, and lemon juice together.
3. Slowly drizzle in olive oil while whisking to emulsify.
4. Stir in Worcestershire and finely grated Parmesan. Season.
5. Toss romaine with dressing.
6. Top with croutons and shaved Parmesan.
tips: The anchovies melt into the dressing — you won't taste fish, just deep savory flavor. Add dressing just before serving to keep lettuce crisp.

name: Roasted Brussels Sprouts
description: Roasted until caramelized and crispy on the outside, tender within. Finished with a drizzle of balsamic glaze and a handful of Parmesan.
ingredients:
- 500g Brussels sprouts, halved
- 3 tbsp olive oil
- Salt and pepper
- 2 tbsp balsamic glaze
- Optional: Parmesan, crispy bacon, pomegranate seeds
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 220°C.
2. Toss Brussels sprouts with olive oil, salt, and pepper.
3. Arrange cut-side down in a single layer on a baking sheet.
4. Roast 20–25 minutes without stirring, until cut sides are deeply golden.
5. Transfer to a plate, drizzle with balsamic glaze.
6. Top with optional Parmesan or extras.
tips: High heat and not overcrowding the pan is the secret. Cut-side down creates the caramelization. Don't stir until the end.

name: Overnight Oats
description: No-cook breakfast made the night before. Creamy, customizable, and genuinely a good morning when you open the fridge to find it already done.
ingredients:
- 60g rolled oats (certified GF if needed)
- 180ml oat milk or almond milk
- 2 tbsp chia seeds
- 1 tbsp maple syrup
- ½ tsp vanilla extract
- Toppings: berries, banana, nut butter, granola
instructions:
1. Combine oats, milk, chia seeds, maple syrup, and vanilla in a jar or container.
2. Stir well to combine.
3. Cover and refrigerate at least 4 hours or overnight.
4. In the morning, stir and add a splash more milk if too thick.
5. Top with your chosen toppings and eat.
tips: The chia seeds are what makes it thick and satisfying — don't skip them. This keeps in the fridge for up to 3 days.

name: Chocolate Lava Cakes
description: Individual chocolate cakes with a molten center — the kind of thing that makes people gasp when you cut into them. Easier than you think.
ingredients:
- 115g dark chocolate (70%+), chopped
- 115g butter
- 2 eggs + 2 egg yolks
- 100g powdered sugar
- 60g all-purpose flour
- Butter and cocoa powder for ramekins
- Vanilla ice cream to serve
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 220°C. Butter 4 ramekins and dust with cocoa.
2. Melt chocolate and butter together. Cool slightly.
3. Whisk eggs, egg yolks, and sugar until combined.
4. Stir in chocolate mixture, then flour.
5. Divide batter into ramekins. Can be refrigerated up to 24 hours at this point.
6. Bake 10–12 minutes — edges set, center still jiggly.
7. Immediately invert onto plates. Serve with ice cream.
tips: 10 minutes is the magic number in most ovens. Better to underbake — you can always put it back in. Test one as a sacrifice before your dinner party.

name: Tacos al Pastor
description: Marinated pork cooked until caramelized, served in small corn tortillas with pineapple, cilantro, and onion. The marinade is where the magic lives.
ingredients:
- 700g pork shoulder, thinly sliced
- 3 dried guajillo chiles, soaked and deseeded
- 2 dried ancho chiles, soaked and deseeded
- 3 cloves garlic
- 1 chipotle in adobo + 1 tbsp sauce
- 1 tsp cumin
- 1 tsp dried oregano
- 3 tbsp white vinegar
- 2 tbsp orange juice
- 150g pineapple, cubed
- Small corn tortillas
- Cilantro, white onion, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Blend soaked chiles, garlic, chipotle, cumin, oregano, vinegar, and orange juice until smooth.
2. Coat pork slices in marinade. Refrigerate at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
3. Cook pork in batches in a very hot cast iron pan until charred in spots.
4. In the last 2 minutes, add pineapple to the pan.
5. Warm tortillas on a dry pan.
6. Assemble tacos: pork, pineapple, cilantro, onion, squeeze of lime.
tips: The char is non-negotiable. Cook in small batches so the pan stays screaming hot — crowding the pan = steaming, not caramelizing.

name: Guacamole
description: Proper guacamole — mashed by hand in a molcajete if you have one, with jalapeño, lime, cilantro, and white onion. Nothing processed, nothing fancy.
ingredients:
- 3 ripe Hass avocados
- 1 jalapeño, seeded and minced
- ¼ cup white onion, finely diced
- ¼ cup cilantro, chopped
- Juice of 1½ limes
- ½ tsp kosher salt
- Optional: 1 ripe tomato, diced
instructions:
1. Halve and pit avocados. Scoop into a bowl.
2. Add salt and lime juice. Mash to desired consistency — chunky is good.
3. Fold in jalapeño, onion, and cilantro.
4. Taste and adjust salt and lime.
5. Add tomato if using.
6. Serve immediately with chips.
tips: Ripe avocados are the only requirement. If making ahead, press plastic wrap directly on the surface to prevent browning.

name: Mexican Street Corn (Elote)
description: Grilled corn on the cob slathered with mayo, cotija cheese, chili powder, and lime. The street food of summer.
ingredients:
- 4 ears of corn, husked
- 4 tbsp mayonnaise
- 4 tbsp sour cream
- 100g cotija cheese, crumbled
- 2 tsp chili powder (or tajín)
- 1 tsp smoked paprika
- Juice of 1 lime
- Cilantro to garnish
instructions:
1. Grill corn over high heat, turning occasionally, until charred in spots — about 10–12 minutes.
2. Mix mayo and sour cream.
3. While corn is hot, brush all over with mayo mixture.
4. Roll in crumbled cotija.
5. Dust with chili powder and paprika.
6. Squeeze lime over everything. Garnish with cilantro.
tips: Char is everything. If you don't have a grill, a screaming hot cast iron pan or your oven broiler works — rotate for even color.

name: Churros
description: Crispy fried churros rolled in cinnamon sugar, served with thick chocolate dipping sauce. The dough comes together in minutes.
ingredients:
- 240ml water
- 2 tbsp sugar
- ½ tsp salt
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- 160g all-purpose flour
- Oil for frying
- 100g sugar + 2 tsp cinnamon for rolling
- Chocolate sauce to serve
instructions:
1. Bring water, sugar, salt, and oil to a boil.
2. Remove from heat, add flour, and stir vigorously until a dough forms.
3. Transfer to a piping bag with a star tip.
4. Heat oil to 180°C.
5. Pipe 12–15cm strips directly into oil. Fry 2–3 minutes per side until golden.
6. Drain on paper towel, immediately roll in cinnamon sugar.
7. Serve with chocolate sauce.
tips: Make sure the oil is at the right temperature — too cool and churros absorb oil, too hot and they brown before cooking through. Eat immediately.

name: Agua de Jamaica
description: Hibiscus flower agua fresca — deep ruby red, tart, and slightly floral. Served cold over ice. One of the most refreshing drinks you can make.
ingredients:
- 40g dried hibiscus flowers (flor de Jamaica)
- 1.5L water
- 100–150g sugar, to taste
- Juice of 1 lime
- Ice to serve
instructions:
1. Bring 500ml water to a boil. Add hibiscus flowers.
2. Simmer for 5 minutes, then steep for another 10.
3. Strain, discarding flowers.
4. Stir in sugar while hot until dissolved.
5. Add remaining 1L cold water and lime juice.
6. Chill and serve over ice.
tips: The concentrate keeps in the fridge for a week — dilute to taste when serving. Adjust sweetness generously; it's meant to be refreshing, not tart.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce — a North African-Middle Eastern staple that's become a weekend morning favorite worldwide.
tagline: Eggs in sauce, the good kind
ingredients:
- 4 large eggs
- 2 cans whole tomatoes (800g total)
- 1 red bell pepper, diced
- 1 yellow onion, diced
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tsp cumin
- 1 tsp smoked paprika
- ½ tsp cayenne
- 1 tsp sugar
- 3 tbsp olive oil
- Salt and pepper
- Feta and cilantro or parsley to serve
instructions:
1. Heat olive oil in a wide oven-safe skillet. Sauté onion and pepper for 7 minutes.
2. Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and cayenne. Cook 1 minute.
3. Add tomatoes, crushing them by hand. Add sugar, salt, and pepper.
4. Simmer 15 minutes until sauce has thickened.
5. Make wells in the sauce and crack eggs into them.
6. Cover and cook on low until whites are set but yolks still runny — about 5–8 minutes.
7. Top with feta, fresh herbs, and serve with bread.
tips: Every shakshuka is different — trust the process. The eggs continue cooking after you remove the pan from heat.

name: Chicken Tikka Masala
description: The beloved curry — charred marinated chicken simmered in a rich, creamy tomato sauce. Takes time but most of it is passive.
ingredients:
- 700g chicken thighs, boneless
- 200ml full-fat yogurt
- 2 tsp garam masala
- 2 tsp turmeric
- 2 tsp cumin
- 2 tsp kashmiri red chili powder
- 4 cloves garlic + 2cm ginger, grated
- 2 cans crushed tomatoes (800g)
- 200ml heavy cream
- 1 large onion, diced
- 3 tbsp butter or ghee
- Salt
instructions:
1. Marinate chicken in yogurt, half the spices, and garlic-ginger paste. At least 1 hour, or overnight.
2. Grill or broil chicken until charred. Let rest, cut into chunks.
3. Sauté onion in butter until deep golden — 20 minutes.
4. Add remaining spices and garlic-ginger paste. Cook 2 minutes.
5. Add tomatoes. Simmer 20 minutes until thick.
6. Add cream. Simmer 5 minutes.
7. Add chicken. Simmer 10 minutes.
8. Serve over basmati rice with naan.
tips: Charring the chicken before adding to the sauce is what gives it the tikka character. Don't rush the onions — the caramelization is the foundation.

name: Hummus from Scratch
description: Silky, cloud-like hummus made with dried chickpeas, tahini, and lemon. The kind of hummus that makes you realize store-bought is a completely different product.
ingredients:
- 400g cooked chickpeas (or 1 can, drained)
- 120ml good tahini
- 3 tbsp fresh lemon juice
- 2 cloves garlic
- 3–4 tbsp ice water
- Salt
- Olive oil, paprika, and parsley to serve
instructions:
1. If using canned chickpeas, save some for topping. Peel chickpeas for smoother hummus (optional but recommended).
2. Add chickpeas to a food processor with tahini, lemon juice, and garlic. Blend 1 minute.
3. With the motor running, add ice water 1 tbsp at a time until silky smooth. Season with salt.
4. Blend for at least 4 minutes total — the long blend is the secret.
5. Transfer to a plate, make a well in the center.
6. Drizzle olive oil, dust with paprika, garnish with parsley and reserved chickpeas.
tips: The ice water is the secret to ethereally smooth hummus. Blend longer than feels necessary. If using dried chickpeas, adding ¼ tsp baking soda to the cooking water softens them dramatically.

name: Spiced Roasted Chickpeas
description: Crispy roasted chickpeas seasoned with cumin, paprika, and cayenne. The ideal snack when you want something crunchy without reaching for chips.
ingredients:
- 2 cans chickpeas, drained and rinsed
- 2 tbsp olive oil
- 1 tsp cumin
- 1 tsp smoked paprika
- ½ tsp cayenne pepper
- ½ tsp garlic powder
- 1 tsp salt
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 200°C.
2. Dry chickpeas thoroughly — spread on a towel and pat completely dry.
3. Toss with olive oil and salt.
4. Spread in a single layer on a baking sheet.
5. Roast 30–35 minutes, shaking the pan every 10 minutes.
6. Remove from oven, immediately toss with spices.
7. Let cool on the pan — they crisp up as they cool.
tips: Moisture is the enemy of crispiness. Dry them as thoroughly as you can before oiling. Season after roasting, not before — salt draws moisture.

name: Chicken Adobo
description: Filipino chicken braised in vinegar, soy sauce, and bay leaves until the sauce reduces to a glossy, intensely savory glaze. The national dish for a reason.
tagline: The best thing vinegar ever did
ingredients:
- 1kg chicken pieces (thighs and legs)
- 120ml white cane vinegar or apple cider vinegar
- 80ml soy sauce
- 8 cloves garlic, lightly smashed
- 3 bay leaves
- 1 tsp whole black peppercorns
- 1 tbsp sugar
- Neutral oil
instructions:
1. Combine vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, bay leaves, peppercorns, and sugar in a bowl.
2. Marinate chicken for at least 30 minutes.
3. Brown chicken in oil over high heat. Set aside.
4. Add marinade to the pot and bring to a boil.
5. Return chicken, reduce heat to low, cover and braise for 30 minutes.
6. Uncover and simmer another 15 minutes, turning chicken occasionally, until sauce reduces to a glaze.
7. Serve over steamed white rice.
tips: The longer it braises, the more tender and flavorful. Leftovers are somehow even better the next day — the flavors deepen overnight.

name: Yuzu Lemonade
description: A bright, citrusy drink made with yuzu juice or yuzu kosho — floral and tart in a way regular lemons can't replicate.
ingredients:
- 4 tbsp yuzu juice (or yuzu bottled juice)
- 2 tbsp lemon juice
- 100g sugar
- 120ml water (for simple syrup)
- 600ml cold sparkling water
- Ice and mint to serve
instructions:
1. Make simple syrup: combine sugar and water, heat until dissolved. Cool.
2. Mix yuzu juice, lemon juice, and cooled simple syrup.
3. Pour over ice in glasses.
4. Top with sparkling water and stir gently.
5. Garnish with mint.
tips: Yuzu juice can be found at Japanese grocery stores. Bottled works well here — no need for fresh. Adjust sweetness to taste.

name: Tres Leches Cake
description: A sponge cake soaked in three types of milk until it's impossibly moist and custardy, topped with whipped cream. The most tender cake you'll ever make.
ingredients:
- 180g all-purpose flour
- 1½ tsp baking powder
- 5 large eggs, separated
- 200g sugar
- 80ml whole milk
- 1 tsp vanilla
- 1 can sweetened condensed milk (397g)
- 1 can evaporated milk (354ml)
- 240ml heavy cream (for soak)
- 360ml heavy cream (for topping)
- 3 tbsp powdered sugar
instructions:
1. Beat egg yolks and sugar until pale. Add milk and vanilla.
2. Fold in flour.
3. Beat egg whites to stiff peaks. Fold into batter.
4. Pour into greased 9x13 pan. Bake at 175°C for 25–30 minutes.
5. While warm, poke all over with a fork.
6. Whisk together condensed milk, evaporated milk, and 240ml cream. Pour slowly over cake.
7. Refrigerate at least 3 hours.
8. Whip remaining cream with powdered sugar. Spread over cake.
tips: Pour the milk mixture slowly in stages so the cake can fully absorb it. This cake is better the next day — make it ahead.

name: Phở Bò
description: The soul of Hanoi in a bowl. A rich, crystal-clear broth built from charred onion and ginger, beef bones, and a bouquet of warm spices — star anise, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom — simmered low and slow for hours. Ladled over silky rice noodles with paper-thin slices of beef that cook in the steaming broth.
ingredients:
- 1.5kg beef bones (knuckle and marrow)
- 500g beef brisket
- 1 large onion, halved
- 8cm piece of ginger, halved lengthwise
- 3 star anise
- 1 cinnamon stick
- 4 whole cloves
- 2 black cardamom pods
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Salt to taste
- 400g dried bánh phở noodles
- 200g beef sirloin, thinly sliced
- Bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime, chili, hoisin, sriracha to serve
instructions:
1. Blanch bones in boiling water for 10 minutes, drain and rinse.
2. Char onion and ginger directly over a flame or under a broiler until blackened in spots.
3. Toast star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom in a dry pan until fragrant, 1–2 minutes.
4. Combine bones, brisket, charred aromatics, and toasted spices in a large pot. Cover with 3 litres of cold water.
5. Bring to a boil, skim foam, then reduce to a bare simmer. Cook for 2.5–3 hours.
6. Remove brisket after 1.5 hours (when tender). Strain broth, season with fish sauce, sugar, and salt.
7. Soak noodles per package, then blanch briefly in boiling water.
8. Divide noodles into bowls. Top with sliced brisket and raw sirloin. Ladle boiling broth over — the heat will cook the sirloin.
9. Serve with garnishes on the side.
tips: Don't rush the broth. The quality of phở lives and dies by how long and gently you simmer it. Skimming the foam in the first 30 minutes makes a clear, clean broth.

name: Bún Bò Huế
description: The fiercer, more complex cousin of phở — born in the royal city of Huế. A lemongrass-scented broth spiked with shrimp paste and dried chili, served with thick round noodles, sliced beef, and pork. Deeply aromatic and unapologetically spicy.
ingredients:
- 1kg pork bones
- 500g beef shank
- 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised and cut into 4cm pieces
- 2 tbsp mắm ruốc (fermented shrimp paste)
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 2 tsp sugar
- 2–3 tsp annatto oil or chili oil
- 3 dried chili peppers
- 1 onion, halved and charred
- 400g bún bò Huế noodles (thick round)
- Garnish: banana blossom, shredded cabbage, mint, perilla, lime, sliced chili
instructions:
1. Blanch pork bones in boiling water 10 minutes. Rinse clean.
2. Char onion directly over flame.
3. Combine bones, beef shank, charred onion, and lemongrass in a pot. Cover with 2.5L water. Bring to boil, skim, then simmer for 1.5 hours.
4. Remove beef shank when tender. Slice.
5. Mix shrimp paste with a ladle of broth until dissolved, then strain into the pot.
6. Season with fish sauce, sugar, and chili oil. Adjust heat and salt.
7. Cook noodles per package. Divide into bowls with sliced beef and pork.
8. Ladle broth over. Serve with garnishes.
tips: The fermented shrimp paste is non-negotiable — it's what gives bún bò Huế its signature depth. Add it gradually and taste as you go.

name: Bánh Mì
description: A French baguette made Vietnamese — crispy and airy, stuffed with a harmony of flavors and textures: rich pâté and mayo, savory pork, pickled daikon and carrot, cool cucumber, fresh cilantro, and a slash of jalapeño. Every bite hits every note at once.
ingredients:
- 2 Vietnamese-style baguettes (or small French baguettes)
- 2 tbsp pork liver pâté
- 2 tbsp Kewpie mayo
- 150g chả lụa (Vietnamese pork roll) or any roast pork, sliced
- 1 small daikon, julienned
- 1 medium carrot, julienned
- 2 tbsp rice vinegar
- 1 tsp sugar
- Pinch of salt
- 4 slices cucumber
- Fresh cilantro
- Jalapeño, sliced
- Soy sauce or Maggi seasoning (optional)
instructions:
1. Pickle daikon and carrot: toss with vinegar, sugar, and salt. Set aside for at least 15 minutes.
2. Slice baguettes lengthwise, not quite all the way through. Toast lightly if desired.
3. Spread pâté on one side, mayo on the other.
4. Layer in pork, pickled vegetables, cucumber, cilantro, and jalapeño.
5. Add a few drops of soy sauce or Maggi if you like. Close and eat immediately.
tips: The bread texture is everything — it should be crackly outside, cottony inside. If you can't find Vietnamese baguettes, look for ones with a thin crust and light crumb.

name: Chả Giò
description: Golden, shattering-crispy spring rolls with a savory pork and vegetable filling. The hallmark of any Vietnamese family gathering — always disappearing faster than they're made. Wrapped in rice paper for the proper crispy-crackly exterior.
ingredients:
- 250g ground pork
- 50g glass noodles (mung bean), soaked and roughly chopped
- 1 medium carrot, grated
- 4 dried wood ear mushrooms, soaked and minced
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 egg
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Pepper to taste
- 20–25 round rice paper wrappers (bánh tráng)
- Neutral oil for deep frying
- Nước chấm for serving: fish sauce, lime, sugar, garlic, chili, water
instructions:
1. Mix pork, noodles, carrot, mushrooms, shallots, egg, fish sauce, sugar, and pepper until combined.
2. Briefly dip a rice paper in warm water until just pliable (not too soft).
3. Place 1.5 tbsp filling near the edge. Fold in sides, roll tightly.
4. Heat oil to 160°C. Fry in batches, turning, 8–10 minutes until deep golden and crunchy.
5. Drain on paper towels. Serve with nước chấm.
6. For nước chấm: dissolve 2 tbsp sugar in 3 tbsp warm water. Add 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 minced garlic clove, 1 sliced chili.
tips: Fry at a lower temperature (160°C) for longer rather than hot and fast — this gives the iconic shatteringly crispy exterior without burning.

name: Bún Thịt Nướng
description: Caramelized grilled pork — smoky, slightly sweet, deeply savory — over a bowl of cool vermicelli, crisp lettuce, cucumber, pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs. Finished with nước chấm and a handful of crushed peanuts. One of the great Vietnamese summer bowls.
ingredients:
- 500g pork shoulder or pork belly, thinly sliced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp honey
- 2 shallots, minced
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 stalk lemongrass, finely minced
- 1 tbsp oil
- 300g dried vermicelli (bún)
- Lettuce leaves, torn
- 1 cucumber, sliced
- Bean sprouts
- Fresh mint, perilla, cilantro
- Pickled daikon and carrot
- Crushed roasted peanuts
- Nước chấm for serving
instructions:
1. Marinate pork: mix fish sauce, sugar, honey, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, and oil. Coat pork and marinate at least 30 minutes (overnight is better).
2. Grill or pan-sear pork over high heat until caramelized and slightly charred, 2–3 minutes per side.
3. Cook vermicelli per package, rinse with cold water.
4. Assemble bowls: noodles on the bottom, topped with lettuce, cucumber, bean sprouts, herbs, pickled veg.
5. Add sliced pork. Drizzle generously with nước chấm. Top with peanuts.
tips: The lemongrass in the marinade is key — don't skip it. Pounding it first before mincing releases more fragrance.

name: Gỏi Cuốn
description: Fresh spring rolls that taste like a garden. Translucent rice paper wrapped around shrimp, pork, vermicelli, and a tumble of fresh herbs — light, cooling, and beautiful. Served with a peanut dipping sauce that you'll want to eat with a spoon.
tagline: Fresh, light, and good for your soul
ingredients:
- 12 round rice paper wrappers (bánh tráng)
- 200g medium shrimp, cooked and halved lengthwise
- 150g pork belly, boiled and sliced thin
- 100g thin vermicelli noodles, cooked
- Butter lettuce leaves
- Fresh mint
- Fresh perilla (tía tô)
- 1 cucumber, julienned
- For peanut sauce: 3 tbsp hoisin sauce, 2 tbsp peanut butter, 1 tbsp lime juice, warm water to thin, crushed peanuts, chili
instructions:
1. Prepare all fillings and have them ready before rolling.
2. Make peanut sauce: whisk hoisin, peanut butter, and lime juice with enough warm water to reach a dippable consistency. Top with peanuts and chili.
3. Fill a wide bowl with warm water. Dip a rice paper wrapper for 5–8 seconds until just pliable.
4. Lay flat. Place a lettuce leaf in the center, then herbs, noodles, pork, and a few shrimp along the bottom third.
5. Fold in the sides, then roll tightly from the bottom up, leaving the shrimp visible through the wrapper.
6. Serve immediately with peanut sauce.
tips: Don't over-soak the rice paper — it continues softening after you remove it from the water. A few seconds is enough.
